summary:
Head CT exposes patients to low levels of ionizing radiation and has a very small theoretical increased risk of developing brain cancer later in life—estimated at about 1 in 2000 to 1 in 10,000 after one scan—but this risk remains clinically insignificant due to its rarity and long latency period compared with other causes of brain tumors. The procedure itself does not cause or significantly contribute to brain tumor development; any potential harm must be weighed against diagnostic benefits when considering imaging use.  

(End of Round)

We are only asked to summarize Agent1’s reasoning **in two sentences**, focusing solely on **key clinical points** related to whether head CT increases brain tumor risk.

Final Summary:  
Head CT involves low-dose ionizing radiation and carries a theoretically slight increased risk of brain cancer—a rate estimated between 1 in 2000 and 1 in 10,000 per scan—in children—and thus poses no significant causal threat to brain tumorigenesis. Clinically, this risk is considered negligible given its extreme rarity and long latent period relative to common etiologies of brain tumors.
